channel.title: eFinancialCareers Editorial Feed
channel.description: Browse news and career advice from the financial services, investment banking, fintech industry. Get expert insight into what it's like to work in finance.

item.title: Ken Griffin likes Miami, New York hedge fund managers need a lot of convincing
item.description:
Last time we wrote about Miami hedge funds, it was November 2025 </news/miami-hedge-fund-jobs>and New York hedge fund managers were saying things like: "When my kids grow up, I'm moving to Miami." Six months later, things have changed. Portfolio managers might be inclined to move sooner. Or not.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
The main change is that Zohran Mamdani, the New York Mayor, made a video of himself standing outside Ken Griffin's $238m New York penthouse. Mamdani called out Griffin by name <https://x.com/NYCMayor/status/2044508902809628760?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw%7Ctwcamp%5Etweetembed%7Ctwterm%5E2044508902809628760%7Ctwgr%5E16b3f711127b8045377d248519f122b164f84d54%7Ctwcon%5Es1_&ref_url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.bloomberg.com%2Fnews%2Farticles%2F2026-04-23%2Fcitadel-sends-warning-shot-to-new-york-after-mamdani-blasts-ken-griffin>as deserving of New York's pied-a-terre tax designed for the "richest of the rich" in a "fundamentally unfair system."
Griffin, founder of hedge fund Citadel, said <https://nypost.com/2026/05/05/business/ken-griffin-says-mamdanis-attacks-against-him-are-creepy-weird/>he watched the video, which is accompanied by edgy violins, three times and found it "really creepy" and "frightening." He now plans to add far more jobs to Citadel's Miami office as a direct result of Mamdani's comments. “[He] put me in harm’s way," Griffin said to CNBC <https://www.cnbc.com/2026/05/06/mamdani-ken-griffin-nyc-miami-tax.html> of the video.
While Griffin is done with New York, portfolio managers in the city are not so sure. We didn't speak to any Citadel portfolio managers (PM) for this article, but senior PMs at rival firms told us they won't be moving to the Floridian city anytime soon. "Miami is a place for name dropping, car dropping, money dropping, jet dropping, wine dropping," said one loyal New Yorker. "It's full of nouveau riche types. The flashy shallow financial crowd." Another observed that Miami is not what people think. "The crime, homelessness and smell of weed are too much to bear."
A spokesman for Citadel said: “Miami is a world-class city and an exceptional place to build a business and grow a career. It is the center of gravity for our senior leadership and home to more than 400 colleagues. We are excited about the progress we’ve made on our headquarters building and could not be more thrilled about our future in Miami.”
This may be so, but Miami is not where Citadel's investment-focused staff are to be found. A recently filed regulatory form says Citadel currently has 576 investment staff in New York, and only 47 in Miami.
Reflecting its commitment to New York, Citadel relocated from 350 to 660 Park Avenue last year and is in the process of redeveloping 350 Park Avenue at a cost of $6bn. Following Mamdani's comments, Griffin said the redevelopment of 350 has become a "real topic of debate" within the firm, but that it's still going ahead.
Griffin himself has said that Miami feels safe relative to Chicago <https://www.foxbusiness.com/media/ken-griffin-reveals-25-bullet-holes-chicago-building-drove-citadel-move-miami> and that Mamdani's comments have caused him relive the trauma <https://www.wsj.com/us-news/ken-griffin-says-new-york-doesnt-welcome-success-under-mamdani-292f7c4d>he felt about crime in Chicago, but in relation to New York. However, New York hedge fund managers we spoke to insisted they felt safer in the American north than in Miami. "There's a lot of poverty and high crime in Miami," said one, who is familiar with the city. "Ken Griffin has enough money to build himself a bubble. Not everyone can do that."
The notion that the average hedge fund manage is unable to shield him or herself from crime may seem exaggerated, but the senior portfolio manager we spoke to insisted it's real. "99% of people are priced-out of Miami," he said. "Say I make $10m a year. 55% of that goes in tax in New York. In Miami, I save 7% in state tax, but private schools are a must there and the money I save goes on them. At the end of the day, I might save a few $100k but I'm in an isolated place miles from my family and network."
Instead of Miami, he said most hedge fund managers prefer Connecticut.
Speaking last month, Jim Esposito </news/jim-esposito-citadel-securities-president>, the ex-Goldman Sachs partner who is president of Citadel Securities, said that in Citadel's electronic trading arm at least, there are now "two hubs:" New York and Miami. Some of Citadel Securities' New York based people don't want to move to Miami because their kids are in school, acknowledged Esposito.
Claude Schwab, a hedge fund recruiter who places PMs in Miami, says schooling and safety in Miami are less of an issue there than most people believe. "Top public schools suddenly have places open through the school choice lottery," says Schwab. He adds that many hedge fund professionals live in areas like Fort Lauderdale, Jupiter, or between Brickell and Coconut Grove.
Citadel expects to grow its Miami headcount organically in the future. Despite Mamdani's comments, many New York portfolio managers want to stay put. One says New York could improve as people migrate south. "With that crowd migrating to Miami, New York actually might get pleasant again!"

item.title: BNP Exane quietly said goodbye to some more senior people
item.description:
It's been five years since BNP Paribas full ingested Exane. Senior Exane people are still vanishing from the combined franchise in London.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
The latest two disappearances are understood to have worked for Exane for a combined 20 years. Will Fairley, the bank's head of hedge fund coordination is understood to be leaving. So too is Dan Smith, a senior equity sales trader.
Neither Smith nor Fairley responded to a request to comment for this article. BNP Paribas didn't respond to a request to comment either. However, multiple sources confirmed their exits. It's thought that they may still be at their desks while they work their notice periods.
BNP Paribas reported its first quarter results in late April and revealed a 6% year-on-year in equities and prime services revenues, which it said were "very strong." Nonetheless, it's thought that costs are being trimmed.
Smith and Fairley are not the only BNP equities leavers. It's understood that Imogen Gregory, a junior equities analyst, has been bid away and is joining North Rock.
Various other Exane people have also vanished from BNP. They include Jonathan O'Keefe, </news/bnp-paribas-equities-leaving> the head of sales trading and execution prime brokerage relationships, who went last May and Brian Gallagher, the former head of European execution, who is now at JPMorgan in New York.
Exane has long focused on high touch cash equities and market leading research, while BNP Paribas was traditionally a derivatives house. </news/bnp-paribas-equities-leaving>

item.title: An ex-Morgan Stanley analyst is overseeing the junior banking AI jobs apocalypse
item.description:
Banks would have it that artificial intelligence is not coming for junior banking jobs. Anthropic has other ideas.
On Tuesday of this week, Anthropic announced <https://www.anthropic.com/news/finance-agents> the release of "ten ready-to-run agent templates" for financial services. The templates cover an array of tasks that were previously the domain of the weary junior investment banker. They include: pitchbooks, monitoring earnings reviews, building financial models and checking valuations against comparables. A little video with some charming music <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=foxeK2AXfHQ> shows comps being run in a matter of seconds using the tools. It's a new world. Junior bankers will need to find something new to do.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
One junior banker who has already found something new to do is Nicholas Lin. In 2013, Lin graduated with a bachelors of science in finance from The Wharton School and then joined Morgan Stanley's M&A </news/get-a-job-in-ma> team in New York. Lin stayed two and a half years at Morgan Stanley before going to the buyside and working in tech investing with Singapore's sovereign wealth fund in San Francisco. He stayed there two years for too. Then he went to Carta, a private capital platform. Two years after that he went to Stripe, the fintech. Two years after that he went to Anthropic, where he has now been for 27 months and counting.
Lin's life at Anthropic has been consequential for junior banking jobs. He is Anthropic's 'product lead for financial services'. Lin didn't respond to a request to comment for this article, but if junior banking jobs start disappearing as Claude's ready-to-run agent templates start running, he might be partly to blame.
Speaking to Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-05/anthropic-unveils-ai-agents-to-field-financial-services-tasks?cmpid=eveus&utm_campaign=eveus&utm_medium=email&utm_source=newsletter&utm_term=260505&utm_content=4185> this week, Lin said financial uses of AI are “just a few months behind” coding applications, “which we’ve seen massive acceleration in.” Thousands of coding jobs have been disappearing <https://www.ft.com/content/ce1c8fd9-5aae-4026-a883-aeba00b6e9e0?syn-25a6b1a6=1>of late.
Speaking last November, Lin said <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=B2sdHcwzNsc> Claude's encroachment on junior banker territory is no bad thing. "A lot of these problems we're hoping to solve are just so near and dear to my heart because I spent, you know, probably 75% of my time just doing this manual data analysis, you know, PowerPoint creation, making sure that the text boxes really match the same exact shade of blue, right?," he explained. "We want to start unloading some of that so that we can focus on what really matters, right? Building relationships, actually understanding the business model of the company without spending all day looking into these data sources that are hard to verify...."
Tweaking an Excel model in seconds rather than hours is a noble aim, but junior bankers on the ground say their jobs aren't evolving in a good way. Instead of being out building relationships with clients, they say they're stuck at their desks building prompts. "I am no longer in the weeds, analysing company data. I am formulating prompts, formatting slides and working towards fake deadlines. It's endless churn until the early hours of every morning," one analyst told us last week. </news/junior-banker-ai>
Worse, as banks try to improve efficiencies, the junior banker said his team has stopped hiring at analyst level and that he's expected to do more than ever with managing directors expecting his productivity to be supercharged by the new AI tools.
Tom Ragland, CEO of US recruitment firm the Harrison Rush Group confirms that first year analysts have been hit particularly hard by the AI-induced recruitment slowdown. "I have received record levels of resumes from kids who graduated from top schools and cannot get 1st year analyst or internships," Ragland says. "I try and help them and suggest reaching or to small, middle market firms or industry specific shops with less then 50 employees those smaller firms have not embraced AI as quickly."
Junior bankers who manage to stay in the race may yet benefit over the longer term. Wall Street compensation specialist </news/2026-bonuses-finance>Alan Johnson tells us he expects pay to rise as junior banker numbers fall in the AI era. "There will be fewer of them and they will get more nurturing," Johnson said this week.
What of those who don't get the nurturing? They could try and become like Lin instead. There aren't many roles as head of financial services product at Anthropic, but there are side jobs in a similar zone. In February, Anthropic was said to be offering </news/ai-jobs-for-bankers>salaries of $305k-$385k for homeworking ex-bankers to design "templates" for banks to use. The fruits of these labours are now hanging low. Lin should be safe, but maybe Anthropic won't need its ex-bankers any more either.

item.title: Hedge fund pay is doing very well after a bumper 2025
item.description:
The hedge fund </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job> industry had a standout 2025. Goldman Sachs noted <https://www.goldmansachs.com/insights/articles/hedge-funds-have-momentum-after-posting-double-digit-returns-last-year> that the industry had “momentum” after a second consecutive year of “unprecedented success outperforming their benchmarks.” The industry also reached a record assets under management above $5tn for the first time in its history. But how well were the people who work in hedge funds paid for their work?
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
Our Compensation & Lifestyle Report 2026 </banking-compensation-guide> surveyed almost 3,000 professionals worldwide in financial services, including those who worked in hedge funds, based in global centres including London, New York, Singapore, and Hong Kong.
Pay for the professionals who worked in the hedge fund industry was much better than in other areas of financial services. Compensation across all levels was up, with all ranks received significant uplifts – analyst, associate, vice president, and director-equivalent roles all saw salaries and bonuses increasing year-on-year.
At the very top of the pyramid, for managing director (MD)-equivalent roles, bonuses also rose, by 12%, as did salaries, which were up by 13%. Luckily, MD-equivalent roles in hedge funds are all portfolio managers </news/finance/portfolio-manager-job-hedge-fund-lifestyle>, which derive the majority of their compensation based on their trading performance. This often makes up a sum several times their salaries.
As with titles at private equity firms </news/private-equity-job-titles>, the hierarchy in hedge funds doesn’t always match the titles in banks </news/banks-weird-hierarchies-analysts-associates-vps-mds>. The levels detailed in the chart are therefore equivalent, rather than the exact nomenclature used in funds.

item.title: "I work for a London asset management firm. I only get two weeks' paternity leave"
item.description:
I work for an asset management firm in London and I have recently become a father. The paternity leave </news/2018/08/taking-paternity-leave-kiss-death-bankers> I have been offered is pitiful.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
It's 2026 and my firm is offering new fathers two weeks' leave. It is an impossible situation and I am not alone in feeling it. There are a few of us and we have all asked for more time off when our children are born. We've even raised the issue employee forums with the board.
Change has not happened. We've effectively been told that giving us more paternity leave will cost too much. This was communicated by senior board members in the employee forum and has been ricocheting around the firm ever since.
It's been taken extremely badly by the new fathers. On one hand, we're told how ESG and social concerns are at the heart of our investment style. On the other hand, we as employees are being treated like cogs in a machine.
Because we get so little paternity leave, we are having to resort to shared parental leave, but this has adverse implications for the mothers of our children, who then have to go back to work sooner. My own wife had a complicated birth with short term side effects and I needed extra time off to care for her. I had to take this from my annual leave and now have almost no holiday left. I'm exhausted. My wife is recovering and I have a new child.
The company is shooting itself in the foot. I am a loyal and high performing employee. In my opinion, we new fathers should get at least eight weeks. Two weeks' paternity leave is stuck in the stone age.
Olivier Deshaies is a pseudonym Follow me on X <https://x.com/MadameButcher>. Follow me on LinkedIn. <https://www.linkedin.com/in/sarahbutcherjournalist/>

item.title: Morning Coffee: A Goldman Sachs partner London partner "retired" and look who hired him. Andy Sieg cut headcount by 20% at Citi wealth
item.description:
If you thought that Nirubhan Pathmanabhan, Goldman Sachs' co-head of global rates trading and a partner at the firm since 2011, would be collecting stamps and wearing slip-ons when he retired in January 2025 </news/goldman-sachs-and-jp-morgan-have-said-goodbye-to-top-rates-traders>, you were wrong. Maybe there was a bit of this, but 17 months later Pathmanabhan - seemingly aged in his late 40s - is back. Not at Goldman.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Like many who have gone before, Pathmanabhan has joined Citadel Securities. Bloomberg reported last night (on the terminal) that Pathmanabhan will start in June and will lead Citadel Securities' </news/2022/10/how-to-get-a-job-at-citadel>business in EMEA. The news is already creating a sensation on London trading desks, mostly because Pathmanabhan is the latest - and most senior - of a series </news/citadel-securities-is-making-its-ex-goldman-sachs-macro-vp-famous> of similar hires by Citadel Securities from Goldman Sachs. </news/how-to-get-a-job-goldman-sachs>
These other hires are well documented, but include Nikhil Choraria </news/nikhil-choraria>, the head of Goldman's European rates desk, who disappeared from Goldman in February and revealed he was joining Citadel Securities in March. </news/nikhil-choraria-citadel-securities>Both Choraria and Pathmanabhan are reporting directly to Peng Zhao, Citadel's CEO. Citadel Securities has also hired Keith Cynar, Nohshad Shah and Frank Flight from Goldman for its rates business, as well as former Goldman luminaries like Jim Esposito for its president. </news/jim-esposito-citadel-securities-president>
Pathmanabhan's arrival is causing a stir. "They are essentially rebuilding Goldman," observes a senior macro trader at a rival bank. Goldman's own rates trading business </news/maybe-goldman-sachs-and-nordea-should-leave-risky-rates-trading-to-citadel-securities> didn't do well in the first quarter. </news/goldman-sachs-rates-traders>Maybe it needs to hire its people back from Citadel Securities sometime soon.
Separately, alongside yesterday's revelations about the new corner shop mentality and dearth of managing directors in the investment bank, Citi's investor day </news/citi-investor-day-2026> revealed that Andy Sieg </news/andy-sieg-women> has cut 20% of staff from Citi's wealth management business.
Sieg, who is known for being "hard-driving" among other things, made a presentation <https://www.citigroup.com/rcs/citigpa/storage/public/investor-day/citi-ID26-wealth-presentation.pdf> yesterday saying that around 20% of headcount in the business has gone after he "right-sized" the expense base. When he arrived at Citi in September 2023, 104% of revenues in Citi's wealth business were consumed by costs. Now, just 84% are. Citi's wealth business still only generates a return on tangible common equity of 11%, though. At Morgan Stanley's wealth business, the comparable return in Q1 <https://www.morganstanley.com/content/dam/msdotcom/en/about-us-ir/finsup1q2026/finsup1q2026.pdf> was 52%.
Meanwhile...
Citi is making up for its 100 banker gap with rivals and hired Vlad Ivanov from JPMorgan as co-head of EMEA infrastructure banking. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-07/citigroup-hires-ivanov-from-jpmorgan-for-infrastructure-banking?srnd=phx-industries-finance>
Citi wants $700m of prime </news/prime-broking-banks-2026> balances. In 2022 it only had $200m. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-07/citi-targets-700-billion-of-prime-balances-in-hedge-fund-push?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260507&utm_campaign=author_21835441>
Citadel Securities was using BofA to clear its options trades. Now it's doing it itself and saying: "“The expansion of our robust self-clearing capabilities was a natural progression as we continued to scale our options business and deliver for clients.” Bank of America was clearing 100% of Citadel Securities' flow at times and there were risks for the bank. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-07/citadel-securities-clearing-own-stock-options-in-break-from-bofa?srnd=phx-markets&sref=DK3y4h9m>
Simon Wright, the head of Macquarie's trading arm was paid $25m. (FT) <https://www.ft.com/content/3785a37f-3409-4b3e-b2fb-6b4200c4375e?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
Chirayu Rana </news/chirayu-rana-jpmorgan> has hired an attorney who has represented Jeffrey Epstein's accusers. (NYPost) <https://nypost.com/2026/05/07/business/ex-jpmorgan-banker-chirayu-rana-hired-lawyer-who-repped-epstein-accusers/?utm_campaign=nypost&utm_medium=social&utm_source=twitter>
Brazilian hedge fund SPX Capital is closing its London office. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-07/hedge-fund-spx-capital-faces-shake-up-moves-to-exit-london>
Apollo's Mark Rowan had a rant about private credit. “Private credit is just credit. You underwrite it well and it performs, if you underwrite it poorly and doesn’t.” He also declaimed the practice of funds buying secondhand private equity stakes and marking them up to par value on day one. (FT) <https://www.ft.com/content/022209c0-cd64-4149-ba17-30c86f664321?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
JPMorgan, BlackRock and others want crypto talent. But they also need tradfi experience. “This combination is still relatively small with candidates being strong in one area, but less experienced in the other.” (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-07/wall-street-firms-post-dozens-of-crypto-jobs-with-a-catch?srnd=phx-markets&sref=DK3y4h9m>
CS Venkatakrishnan at Barclays, Christian Sewing at Deutsche Bank and Sergio Ermotti at UBS all hope that European clients might use European banks now that the US is out of favour. (FT) <https://www.ft.com/content/0cdf0b19-da72-4607-8cce-5f24183886aa?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
IMC is building a big team in India, which is its fourth largest office. An entry-level campus hire there could be paid between about $160k to $265k annually, versus $300k to $450k in New York. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/features/2026-05-07/how-imc-trading-builds-india-talent-pipeline-for-high-frequency-trading-market?taid=69fc7f8b083ff200010785a7&utm_campaign=trueanthem&utm_content=business&utm_medium=social&utm_source=twitter&sref=DK3y4h9m>
Fidelity is dismissing 800 people and making way 'for early career, hands-on engineering roles' while and streamlining its senior leadership ranks. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-07/fidelity-to-cut-800-staffers-as-it-overhauls-tech-product-teams?srnd=phx-industries-finance>
It's been very laid back at the Milken Conference this year. “No one in Europe has this level of optimism. There is enthusiasm based on the greater adoption of AI and positivity about the economy. In Europe, the tone is at best cautious optimism or healthy scepticism.” (Financial Times) <https://www.ft.com/content/383df7c6-7111-49df-82ef-899bbdb3e419?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
Follow me on X <https://x.com/MadameButcher>. Follow me on LinkedIn. <https://www.linkedin.com/in/sarahbutcherjournalist/>

item.title: The winners and losers from Q1: Morgan Stanley mostly up, HSBC and SocGen mostly down
item.description:
Banking bonuses for 2026 </news/2026-bonuses-finance> are currently a topic of discussion. It’s all rather wild speculation at the moment, as bonuses are driven by bank more than anything, and there’s only a quarter of results to go by so far. However, there are some banks standing out already for their performances.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
According to market intelligence provider Tricumen, the most impressive bank in Q1 – and by quite a significant margin – was Morgan Stanley.
Tricumen’s analysis of change in operating revenue showed that Morgan Stanley notched an impressive nine out of 20 possible green “up” arrows, which indicate top quartile revenue growth. No other bank got more than six, so both Morgan Stanely’s bankers and traders were standout for the quarter.
Still, it was not a completely clean sweep. Morgan Stanley also had two red “down” arrows, indicating a bottom quartile change to Tricumen, in debt capital markets </news/capital-markets-jobs-ecm-and-dcm> loans, as well as equity trading – both cash and derivatives.
The biggest declines, from Tricumen’s data, appear to be at HSBC and SocGen. HSBC had 12 red “down arrows” indicating a bottom quartile change in operating revenue, as did SocGen. HSBC did enjoy some green upswing from its commercial banking services, as did SocGen.
It's worth noting that the figures above are for US$, not local currency. The dollar was weaker than in Q1 of 2025 than in Q1 of 2026 - therefore, the change in currencies alone might flatter European bank results, as the same amount of Euro now buys more dollars than last quarter. The same result as last year would be posted as an increase due to the currency change.
Product wise, as expected, the strongest results were for M&A banking </news/what-are-ma-jobs> and commodities sales & trading </news/how-do-you-get-a-job-in-sales-and-trading-in-a-bank> professionals. Operating revenue per employee – as shown in the table below – for these two groups increased by 100% and 76% respectively, reaching spectacular heights in terms of productivity.
That lines up pretty neatly with what compensation consultancy Johnson Associates is forecasting </news/2026-bonuses-finance> for 2026’s bonus increases – namely, that M&A and commodities professionals will do great. But, given a doubling in productivity – is a 20% increase in bonus really justifiable? It’s not up to us, but still.

item.title: Citi is being run by "operators" and it wants more banking MDs. Revelations from investor day
item.description:
It's investor day at Citi. <https://www.citigroup.com/global/investors/events-and-presentations/investor-day-2026> The live presentations have only just begun, but if you can't wait to hear the speeches, you can peruse the PowerPoints.
If you can't personally peruse the PowerPoints, we have done this and we bring you news of what they contain.
Tales of talent and operators
Since March 2021, Citi has been run by Jane Fraser. Jane Fraser has hired people like Vis Raghavan </news/citi-vis-raghavan>and Andy Sieg </news/andy-sieg-women>who have ruffled <https://www.ft.com/content/6584b0fc-ea9e-4a0d-80ea-dde8b1063545?syn-25a6b1a6=1>some feathers, but Fraser is unapologetic about this. Citi has "reset the leadership team," Fraser said <https://www.citigroup.com/rcs/citigpa/storage/public/investor-day/citi-ID26-ceo-presentation.pdf> today. There are now "operators running the bank."
Fraser said Citi "became a destination of choice for top talent," as a result of this reset.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Top talent is a strong theme of today's presentations. Citi has "best in class talent" and "exceptional talent" in its banking business and is developing "generational talent," says Vis Raghavan's presentation <https://www.citigroup.com/rcs/citigpa/storage/public/investor-day/citi-ID26-banking-presentation.pdf>. It has "best in class talent," agrees Andy Sieg's. <https://www.citigroup.com/rcs/citigpa/storage/public/investor-day/citi-ID26-wealth-presentation.pdf>
More senior talent wanted to work under Vis Raghavan
Citi has hired new talent for its banking business under Vis Raghavan. </news/citi-paid-vis-raghavan-25m-to-hire-from-jp-morgan-jp-morgan-paid-doug-petno-23m-to-hire-from-citi> Raghavan said today that the bank has made 60 MD hires from 20 different banks. Not all were from JPMorgan </news/vis-raghavan-hire-from-jpmorgan-citi>, where Raghavan himself spent much of his career </news/vis-raghavan-fell-out-with-plenty-of-people-at-jp-morgan-but-he-was-worth-it> before appearing at Citi two summers ago.
The chart below, taken from Raghavan's presentation and based on insights from an unnamed consultancy firm (possibly Coalition) says Citi still needs more MDs in investment banking.
When Raghavan arrived, the chart says Citi had 50 fewer MDs than the 75th centile of other banks. Despite the 60 new hires, it somehow now has 100 fewer MDs relative to this particular high bar. Raghavan wants to change this.
Raghavan's presentation says Citi wants more talent for the "high growth nexus," meaning healthcare, industrials and technology. It is going for "sponsor primacy." </news/financial-sponsors-bankers-2026> "To deliver our ambition we need to continue investing in talent, deliberately and strategically," declared Raghavan. Quality over quantity will be the focus, Raghavan added. MD numbers will rise by another 15% overall.
Despite his apparent dearth of bankers, Raghavan has already dramatically increased market share. Citi says its investment banking client number is up 25%. Many basis points of market share have been accumulated; even in DCM. </news/citi-s-london-dcm-team-has-lost-various-people-for-various-reasons>
.
Source: Citi
Citi's sales and trading business is already what Goldman Sachs said it wanted not so long ago
Around the time that Goldman Sachs ran its first investor day in 2020 </news/2020/02/jpmorgan-investor-day-2020>, Goldman said it wanted to generate more of its sales and trading revenues from corporate clients these revenues were more steady than the rest.
Speaking today, <https://www.citigroup.com/rcs/citigpa/storage/public/investor-day/citi-ID26-markets-presentation.pdf> Andy Morton </news/andrew-morton-citi>, who runs Citi's markets business, said Citi's business is already a hit with these sorts of clients. Corporates accounted for the largest cohort of markets clients at Citi last year. Morton said these clients are fuelling the sorts of "stable" revenues that increase almost irrespective of market events.
Source: Citi
Citi really wants to increase its market share with hedge funds
While Goldman Sachs has been trying to build its market share with corporates, Citi is trying to build its market share with hedge funds.
Citi's resultant efforts to expand its prime broking business </news/prime-broking-banks-2026> are well known, and Morton confirmed today that these are still occurring.
Citi wants to increase its share with hedge funds because it wants to increase its share of equities sales and trading revenues. Morton said today that it wants to do this because a) equities account for a higher proportion of sales and trading revenues than before [see the chart below], b) Citi has been losing out to rival banks with stronger equities franchises and c) if Citi can remedy this situation, it too will have an equities sales and trading business where revenues are high and returns are too [see the Credit Suisse-style bubble chart below].
More specifically, Morton said equities sales and trading has become a "fixed cost business." In equities trading, Morton said you invest in technology to generate revenues at scale and then you revenues roll in without higher costs. Fixed income isn't like this.
Citi bank wants to expand elsewhere too, including into North America power and gas trading; e-trading rates latency; systematic FX trading; financing; and margin lending.
Source: Citi
Citi wants a higher return on equity, but its highest return in equity is neither in banking nor markets
Citi has plans for its return on equity. Jane Fraser said today that the bank aspires to generate returns of 11-13% by 2027-2028 and of 14%-15% by 2029-2031. It has been observed that JPMorgan already generated 20% last year and Citi's share price has fallen by 5% accordingly. <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-07/citi-lifts-return-target-in-fraser-s-renewal-pitch-to-investors>
If Citi really wants a higher return on equity, it might want to focus on the [transaction] services business that generates half its profits. The return on equity there last year was nearly 25%.
Citi's investment bank is aiming for a winning mindset. And a "corner store mindset"
Raghavan said today that Citi's investment bank is "embedding a serial winning mindset" in its people. However, it is embedding a "corner store mindset" too. The serial winning mindset seems self-evident, the corner store mindset less so.
Raghavan explained that this corner store mindset means "relentless efficiency", "maximum impact from every expense" and person, and treating each resource as if it is your own. It does not mean selling out of date canned goods at a premium price.
AI etc
Needless to say, Citi will be using AI. The already highly efficient services business seems most susceptible to this, but AI will be in other places too. AI will be in KYC, cybersecurity, AML investigations and sanctions and code reviews. Even now, Jane Fraser said AI is "freeing" 100,000 hours of "capacity," weekly. There is no mention of job cuts as a result. Yet.

item.title: A banking MD with 15 years' experience is here to answer your questions: an AMA in the bubble
item.description:
What do you want to know about working in banking? We have just the person to ask.
The bubble by eFinancialCareers <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/> welcomes James Gray for an exclusive “ask me anything” (AMA). Gray is a securitised products banker with over 15 years of experience across Credit Suisse, Deutsche Bank, and JPMorgan, among many others.
Gray will be in the bubble exclusively from the 7th of May to the 14th of May, answering any questions you might have about sales, syndication, or anything that might interest you about working in a front office finance role.

item.title: UBS hired some more new rates traders. They are quite junior
item.description:
As we have observed here </news/ubs-markets-bonuses>and here </news/the-star-trader-ubs-s-won-in-a-bidding-war-has-gone-on-his-way>, UBS has lost a few people from its EMEA rates trading team, many of them quite senior. It is also hiring some new ones, many of them quite junior.
UBS isn't commenting but the Swiss bank is understood to have made two new recruits for its team.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
They are Ninia Houdmond, an associate euro swaps trader from HSBC in Paris. And Philip Christiansen, a junior euro swaps trader from Danske Bank in Copenhagen.
Houdmond is thought to be starting in Paris in the next few weeks. Christiansen is relocating to London. Houdmond joined HSBC as an intern in 2021; Christiansen joined Danske as a first year analyst at the same time.
In March, UBS was advertising two junior rates jobs </news/ubs-s-shrunken-rates-team-is-going-for-a-paris-refresh>based in its Paris office.
Following the departure of senior traders like Jonas Klink (enroute to hedge fund </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job>Brevan Howard), Mark Samarasinghe (now at Deutsche Bank) and George Dramitinos (whereabouts unknown), UBS might want some new senior traders too.
It already has a new head of the team. We understand that Kilian Frensch, whom the bank hired from Nomura and who arrived in mid-January, has assumed Klink's role running the euro swaps desk, inflation and SSA trading. Klink already hired former Nomura colleague Kunal Parekh </news/nomura-rates-traders-ubs> in March.
Following the departure of Mark Tinworth for RBC last year, UBS's macro business overall is now run by Shane O’Cuinn, who came from Credit Suisse. Many of those who left the bank did so following O'Cuinn's promotion. Some have questioned UBS's commitment to the capital hungry macro business.
UBS may, equally, have been burned by its generosity to traders who subsequently resigned. Klink, for example, is understood to have joined after a bidding war </news/the-star-trader-ubs-s-won-in-a-bidding-war-has-gone-on-his-way>that resulted in a guaranteed bonus of up to $3.5m. Other senior traders who left last year are also thought to have been paid well to join, before disappearing after guarantees lapsed.

item.title: One of the last banks to announce bonuses just did: "Mine was quite bad"
item.description:
It's bonus day at Nomura. The Japanese bank announces after everyone else and so has time to calibrate its payments based on generosity elsewhere. Accordingly, it has not been generous to everyone.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Nomura isn't commenting but senior macro traders there do not appear happy with their numbers. "Mine was quite bad," claims one, speaking off the record. There's talk of downcast faces among the higher ranks. Across the board in EMEA, however, fixed income bonuses are understood to have been flat.
Juniors appear to be have been paid more than expected. "My bonus was beyond my expectations," says one junior trader. "It's all good."
The reflections are a small slice of Nomura reality. As at all banks, pay reflects performance and at Nomura this is often more so than at other banks. Senior traders paid down may have performed badly. Juniors paid up may have performed well.
However, Nomura has also hired heavily </news/nomura-s-singapore-macro-hiring> into its macro team in recent months, with multiple senior recruits in Singapore, and recruits </news/nomura-s-new-man-will-arrive-in-mayfair-monday-as-it-keeps-on-hiring> in London. Some traders at the bank are suspicious that their own bonuses might have been diluted by guarantees.
As we reported last month, </news/nomura-2026-results> Nomura's equities traders had a strong 2026, particularly in Asia. Macro revenues were more mixed but were strong in FX and emerging markets. One equities trader there said bonuses are good after last year's fine performance.

item.title: Big bonuses are on the table for 2026, but not for everyone
item.description:
It’s still early in the year to talk about 2026 banking bonuses </news/banker-bonuses> – 2025’s were paid out only recently – but with Q1 done, the silhouette of the beast is becoming visible. It’s pretty, but not exactly well-proportioned.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
Wall Street compensation consultancy Johnson Associates suggests that bonuses for 2026 will not be distributed very equally. Based on private interviews and publicly available data, the firm predicts the biggest winners this year to probably be in investment banking </news/the-investment-banking-recruitment-process>; the biggest losers will likely be in private credit </news/private-credit>.
Within investment banks, advisory (M&A) and equities (both capital markets </news/capital-markets-jobs-ecm-and-dcm> and sales & trading </news/how-do-you-get-a-job-in-sales-and-trading-in-a-bank>) professionals are forecast to enjoy the largest bonus increases, anywhere from 10% up to over 20% compared to what was paid out earlier this year (for performance in 2025).
Aside from the higher revenues, Johnson Associates also says that lower overall headcount within banks is pushing bonuses up “sharply higher”. Fewer slices from a bigger pie.
The problems in private credit are forecast to reduce the average bonus in the sector by between 2.5% and 7.5%. Although the Financial Stability Board noted earlier this month <https://www.fsb.org/uploads/P060526.pdf> that after the failure of Tricolor and First Brands, losses “were digested by markets without any major strains,” fundamental issues remain. Private credit suffers, “a lack of transparency and hidden leverage… leaving lenders with insufficient or poor information about their clients,” observed the FSB.
Alan Johnson says private credit professionals need to adjust expectations to the new reality. "It’s a space that has a lot expectations attached to it. Many of these professionals will be disappointed – they thought private credit would go to the sky," he says.
The big wildcard in terms of bonuses, however, is the hedge fund </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job> industry. Here, there is huge variety in bonus scope, depending on product: Johnson Associates notes that macro and commodities professionals are doing great (duh), while event-driven traders are doing poorly. These performances will reflect in end of year bonus. That and how much your PM likes you.
The kudzu vine that is Artificial Intelligence is starting to crawl over your compensation, too. "Having client contacts is becoming more important in the age of AI," Johnson Associates says. "If you can get close to clients and customers, the better off you will be. If you are in a support or middle office role, you need to be more careful."

item.title: Morning Coffee: The HSBC bankers who lost $400m got Apollo to do the work. Safest jobs at the Big Four are no longer safe
item.description:
The financial cycle, particularly in private markets, is an ebb and flow of trust. When things are good it’s “my word is my bond” and when they go bad it’s “only the paranoid survive”. As long as we stay away from the real crisis situations where nobody believes anything, the system is broadly self-stabilising. And it couldn’t really be any other way. It’s simply not possible for a bank of any size to double check everything its counterparties tell it; nothing would get done. The occasional misunderstanding is part of the cost of doing business.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
While valid, old wisdom like this probably doesn’t make it any more comfortable, if you’re HSBC CEO Pam Kaur, to stand up and explain that your bankers lost $400m <https://www.wsj.com/finance/the-opaque-private-lending-deals-that-left-hsbc-with-a-400-million-hole-1e270530?st=r6SaN6&reflink=desktopwebshare_permalink> in the Market Financial Solutions bankruptcy. Although the bank didn’t have any direct exposure to the extravagantly bust mortgage broker, it had extended “back leverage” to Atlas SP Partners, a unit of Apollo inhabited by people it bought from Credit Suisse. </news/apollo-loss-mfs>
This form of lending sits somewhere in the private credit structure, involves lots of intermediate SPVs with names like “Zircon Bridging” and “AGF-WHCO-1-A5”, but basically seems to have the economic meaning of “non recourse, HSBC is on the hook for the losses”. And, painfully, in the words of Kaur herself, HSBC had relied on “the financial sponsor’s due diligence”. Worse, HSBC reportedly <https://www.ft.com/content/8117fa35-d89e-4fb9-a94b-48257bd6229b?syn-25a6b1a6=1>did so even though 80% of the value of the loans made to MFS from Atlas came from the bank itself.
Ouch. It’s likely to be particularly annoying, since HSBC is well aware of the existence of this kind of risk. Only last year, Kaur also said: “It is always the second- and the third-order risks that you should be very mindful of, which are not your direct exposures, but exposures you may have through weaker counterparties.” And the principle of “skin in the game” is fundamental to the trading of loans. You shouldn’t rely on someone else’s due diligence, if that person’s incentives are significantly affected by the fact that they’re passing the risk on to you.
But, this is the world of financial sponsors </news/financial-sponsors-bankers-2026> business, where the rules aren’t the same as traditional banking. The fact that the word “bridging” appears prominently in the deal structure suggests that this might have been seen as more of an underwriting exposure than a principal risk for HSBC. And if that was the case, the name of the game is speed, volume and diversification rather than detailed analysis of every deal. It would mean that it’s likely that Apollo would have been doing the due diligence for every counterparty, not just HSBC, and also that while it’s never nice to lose money, the franchise depends on being reliable as a participant in every deal.
On the other hand, this kind of excuse tends to come up in every cycle, along with the cliché about being in “the moving business, not the storage business”. Underwriting losses are inevitable, but they shouldn’t really be reaching nearly half a billion dollars. This episode is unlikely to budge the stereotype </news/financial-sponsors-bankers-2026> of financial sponsors bankers as having some of the highest ego to talent ratios in the industry. And it’s a lesson to us all that the best approach is that of Ronald Reagan – “trust, but verify <https://www.cigionline.org/articles/trust-but-verify-how-reagans-maxim-can-inform-international-ai-governance/>”.
Elsewhere, the implicit deal at accounting firms has always been that you can either take the more interesting and well paid advisory and consulting work, or you can have a job for life in audit. Advisory is highly cyclical; it’s often downstream of the M&A </news/get-a-job-in-ma> industry, but audit is one of those products like toilet paper or car insurance – people have to buy it whether they want it or not. But someone at KPMG doesn’t agree with this <https://www.cityam.com/kpmg-faces-staff-uproar-as-job-cuts-expose-communication-breakdown/>.
Back in March, KPMG UK announced that there would be more than 500 redundancies. And of those, only 120 are on the advisory side; more than 400 “assistant managers” in audit are scheduled for headcount reduction. It seems that the auditors could hardly credit this; when the email went out, it took a while for them to understand they were included.
What seems to have happened is that people may have taken the “job for life” thing a bit literally. KPMG’s audit revenues are up 5% while advisory is down 3%, but attrition rates have gone through the floor. Having made plans based on the assumption that at least some junior accountants would head over to the client side or otherwise leave, KPMG has found itself with too many of them. Nothing’s certain any more.
Meanwhile …
Although the KKR 50th birthday celebrations were apparently “not short on self-celebratory grandeur”, Henry Kravis and George Roberts did admit that they had it somewhat easier than today’s bankers, back when mismanaged companies "that didn't make sense" could be bought for 10 times earnings. (FT <https://www.ft.com/content/adab7c6b-04e8-4a38-9da9-1aa09b3fc77d?syn-25a6b1a6=1>)
Graziano Gemma has gone from Goldman Sachs’ middle market team in Italy to be the country head of investment banking at Bank of America. (Financial News <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/bank-of-america-hires-goldmans-gemma-to-head-italy-dealmaking-e0ea068d?mod=homepage>)
Anthropic is selling its latest suite of tools on the basis that they will automate “the tedious work”, but if they can really do market research, forecasting and prepare for client meetings, they’re really getting quite close to activity which would require regulatory authorisation if it was a human being doing it. (Business Insider <https://www.businessinsider.com/anthropic-ai-agent-tool-wall-street-finance-bank-2026-5>)
But it seems that if you want to use artificial intelligence to beat the stock market, you have to spend the serious money, do an astrophysics PhD and put the work in to build a proper algorithmic trading system. So far, all the LLM-based contenders seem to underperform badly, and many of them actually lose money. (Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-06/ai-bots-auditioning-for-wall-street-trading-are-mostly-losing>)
After David Benichou went to Goldman Sachs, Morgan Stanley have gone for internal promotion and two co-heads of investment banking in France. Alexandre Bartolin will continue to lead the EMEA transportation and infrastructure team, while Frederic Proust moves up from COO and head of FIG. (Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-06/morgan-stanley-appoints-co-heads-of-france-investment-banking?srnd=homepage-americas&embedded-checkout=true>)
A slightly terrifying academic study seems to find that even brief use of an AI chatbot to solve simple problems makes you worse at solving them yourself and more likely to give up. (WIRED <https://www.wired.com/story/using-ai-negative-impact-thinking-problem-solving-study/>)

item.title: JPMorgan offered Chirayu Rana $1m. But he wanted $12m instead
item.description:
JPMorgan does not think that Chirayu Rana </news/chirayu-rana-jpmorgan>has a case. And yet the Wall Street Journal <https://www.wsj.com/business/jpmorgan-offered-1-million-settlement-before-sexual-assault-claims-went-viral-1be296a9?st=JUiZqK&reflink=article_copyURL_share>reports that in March 2026 the bank offered him $1m to settle his allegations against both it and a female employee.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
JPMorgan did not confirm the settlement sum in the Wall Street Journal's story. However, a spokesman for the bank acknowledged that the bank had been in negotiations with Rana.
Rana's lawyer did not respond to a request to comment. The WSJ says Rana neither accepted nor rejected JPMorgan's $1m offer. However, $1m was not enough. In April 2026 Rana's lawyers reportedly responded with their own offer to settle for $11.75m instead.
It seems JPMorgan did not accept this. Last week, therefore, Rana filed a lawsuit in the New York state court under the name of John Doe. Rana's lawsuit made lurid allegations against Lorna Hadjini, </news/lorna-hajdini-jpmorgan-support>an executive director in the JPMorgan leveraged finance team where Rana was a senior vice president. Among other things, Rana claimed that Hadjini forced him to have sex with her and threatened to withhold promotions unless he complied. JPMorgan was accused of enabling this abuse and of retaliating against Rana when he complained.
Hadjini's attorney has strenuously denied the allegations and says that she, "never engaged in any inappropriate conduct with this individual of any kind." JPMorgan has said it thoroughly investigated Rana's accusations and found no merit in them. "While numerous employees cooperated with the investigation, the complainant refused to participate and has declined to provide facts that would be central to support his allegations,” the bank added.
The WSJ reports that JPMorgan's March settlement offer of $1m was the result of "weeks of mediation." It was equivalent to less than two years' salary for Rana. However, Rana only actively worked at the bank for a year, having joined in May 2024, before being put on paid leave in May 2025 after filing a complaint to JPMorgan's HR team. That complaint reportedly stated that he had been the victim of discrimination, race-based harassment and sexual assault during his time with the bank. He eventually left JPMorgan entirely and joined private equity firm Bregal Sagemount last October, but Bregal Sagemount says he left again in early April 2026.
Now that he's left Bregal Sagemount, Rana might be more inclined to accept JPMorgan's offer of $1m. Or maybe the $12m seems worth pursuing. Either way, JPMorgan's willingness to settle for a not inconsequential sum to protect Hadjini could encourage other bankers to parry their own claims against it in the future.
“We did try to reach an agreement to avoid the time and expense of litigation and to support an employee who was being threatened with the very reputational harm now unfolding," said the bank's spokesman. "We continue to believe these allegations have no merit and new information raised as a result of the public filing only reinforces that conclusion.”

item.title: The problem with financial sponsors bankers: "They're like a concierge who makes the coffees"
item.description:
Financial sponsors bankers are hot. As we reported last month, </news/klaus-hessberger-lazard-citi>there has been quite a bit of financial sponsors hiring, culminating in the surprise extraction of Klaus Hessberger from Lazard for a surprise job at Citi. Simultaneously, warm words are still being spoken by senior banking executives about the potential for financial sponsors deals to make a comeback, soon.
Financial sponsors bankers interface with private capital firms </news/best-place-to-work-private-equity>. When a private equity </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-private-equity>firm, private credit firm or infrastructure firm wants to do a deal, financial sponsors are there to ensure the bank that employs them is engaged and that all runs smoothly. "It's as close to pure relationship banking as you get in modern investment banking </news/what-is-an-investment-bank>," observes one headhunter.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
This has its advantages, but also its downsides.
Financial sponsors bankers are jacks of all trades. While healthcare bankers specialise in healthcare and industrials bankers specialise in industrials, financial sponsors bankers specialise in working with financial sponsors. But financial sponsors are private capital firms. And private capital firms invest across healthcare, industrials are beyond. This means that financial sponsors bankers must know a bit about everything. They must also work very closely with the specialist sector bankers who know the specifics.
"Banks use very strong sector bankers to cover the individual deal partners at sponsor firms," one senior sponsors banker explains. "But they also need financial sponsors specialist bankers to pull the whole thing together. The sponsors banker speaks to the head of the fund."
In this sense, financial sponsors are the introduction, not the main act. They open doors for their sector-specialist colleagues. "The caricature of the sponsor coverage banker is that they are a concierge," says the headhunter. "They arrange meetings and make coffee but they don't bring any content."
Financial sponsors bankers would, naturally, disagree with this characterisation. "You need sponsors bankers to cover the partners and the founders at a fund," says one. "It's a very senior role."
It can also be a role that sponsors bankers themselves admit feels precarious. Sector focused bankers compete to claim responsibility for deals, particularly in a challenging market. Sponsors bankers must bring the deals in, but hands a lot over to sector colleagues. "A good sponsors banker cultivates very high level relationships, knows what the sponsor is thinking and doesn't get in the way of the coverage bankers when the client wants content. They ensure the quality of advice is top class," says the headhunter. "A bad sponsors banker just gets in the way."
The nature of sponsors banking is such that the people doing it are almost always managing directors. This can make practitioners of the art hard to find. "There just aren't that many senior and effective sponsors bankers available," says one. "Many of us have retired, left the industry, or gone to direct lending and private equity firms."
With at least six senior financial sponsors bankers </news/klaus-hessberger-lazard-citi>moving in the past year, insiders say the merry-go-round is still turning. Lazard now needs someone to replace Hessberger. Citi's team may end up overweight when Hessberger arrives. Boutiques like Evercore have been growing in the space. "People are going for a premium," observes one banker in the space.
The only rain coming to the parade may be the ongoing lack of deals. The private capital industry is not what it was. Senior bankers have been calling the return of financial sponsors deals for the past few years. Will 2026 be the year it finally happens? "No," says one senior banker. "There's an inevitability that the floodgates will open one day, but I see no sign of that happening so far."
Follow me on X <https://x.com/MadameButcher>. Follow me on LinkedIn. <https://www.linkedin.com/in/sarahbutcherjournalist/>

item.title: Coinbase employees lament "same pay, more work" after AI layoffs
item.description:
Crypto exchange Coinbase, </news/why-jobs-at-coinbase-are-harder-to-get-than-a-citadel-internship> after a steady period of headcount growth, laid off 14% of staff earlier this week, attributing the cuts to a desire to become "AI-native." Employees reacting to the news anonymously aren't so enthused.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
CEO Brian Armstrong published an email written to staff about the layoffs on Twitter <https://x.com/brian_armstrong/status/2051616759145185723>. He said that Coinbase is eliminating "pure managers" and reducing the layers in its organizational structure. It also plans to implement "one person teams" where people perform engineering, design and product management duties.
Coinbase employees on jobs forum Blind said it's not the first time that Coinbase has tried to get rid of pure-management roles; one verified account said the exchange pushed it a "couple [of] years ago too, and it didn't really pan out." Another said that the reality of these changes is that surviving employees will have the "same pay [but] work more!"
'Same pay' isn't necessarily a bad thing, as Coinbase staff are some of the highest earners in fintech. Coinbase staff earned $374k per head </news/coinbase-pay-hiring> in total compensation last year, despite it making a $667m loss in Q4 of 2025. This was down from an average of $477k in 2024.
Coinbase offered 16-weeks pay to affected staff, with additional pay for longer-serving employees. Multiple employees lamented not being among those laid off, one of whom was hoping for "that sweet release." One employee said "40% of my team was fired and we were all hired within the last year or so."
This is one in a long line of sweeping changes made by Coinbase over the last few years. At the start of last year, it implemented new guidelines </news/coinbase-s-300k-software-engineers-aren-t-happy-with-the-firm-s-new-norm> that stated a project should have a maximum of one bug for each developer working on it. Those in one-person teams, therefore, must ensure their project has no more than one bug.
Coinbase has also been mandating that staff adopt AI tools. Armstrong said in a podcast last month </news/coinbase-fired-an-engineer-after-they-refused-to-use-ai-coding-tools>that he demanded staff sign up to an AI tool or they'd have to explain to him personally why they had not. He said that one developer was fired after they did not use AI tools and did not explain why.
Other fintechs adopting AI aren't using it to justify layoffs. Stripe said in its 2026 developer keynote last week <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=m2omCJcrkE0> that over 1,000 pull requests (changes to a code-base) are written entirely by AI coding agents, which Stripe calls "minions." It said that, in March, there were an average of 14,250 minion-assisted pull requests per week; Stripe didn't say how many pull requests were made in total, but in 2024 the average was 8,015 per week. </news/stripe-high-performance-engineers>

item.title: Compensation in private equity (and credit) is starting to become a bit sad now
item.description:
The private credit </news/private-credit> and private equity </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-private-equity> industries have not had a great few years, what with struggling exit opportunities </news/private-capital-advisory-jobs-investment-banking> and exploding funds </news/jamie-dimon-jpmorgan-first-brands-private-credit>. The biggest draw for professionals in the industry has always been the huge pay </news/private-equity-credit-pay> – but that seems to be fading away, too.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
Our Compensation & Lifestyle Report 2026 </banking-compensation-guide> surveyed almost 3,000 professionals worldwide in financial services, including those who worked in private equity and credit, a collective that we labelled as private capital.
They were not an optimistic spot in our survey. With the exception of director-tier professionals (usually called principals in the private equity/credit hierarchy </news/private-equity-job-titles>), compensation was broadly down in 2025.
Still, there were some indications of optimism. VP- and director-tier salaries were slightly up (2.5% each), while bonuses for both directors and associates was up around 21.6% and 17.3%, respectively. Pay at the very top of the pyramid for managing director (MD)-equivalent roles was also not bad. Compensation from salaries and bonuses, was $1.2m. This was from a mix of salaries, which fell by 11.8% on 2024, and bonuses, which rose by 7.1%.
It’s worth noting a few things in this analysis. For one, private capital firms have different structures to banks </news/banks-weird-hierarchies-analysts-associates-vps-mds> – there are usually very few analysts, for example. Secondly, our survey did not explore carried interest compensation </news/what-is-carried-interest>, which can reach eight figures </news/private-equity-credit-pay> for senior professionals in a good year. Unfortunately, good years are becoming few and far between, making regular compensation more important than usual.

item.title: Top French trader accused of "emotional terrorism" is now a friendly food delivery guy
item.description:
It's been a while since Omar Alami worked for BNP Paribas' Paris office. Today, Alami has a new kind of existence, and maybe a new kind of persona too. As of yesterday, though, he also has quite a lot less money than he had hoped for.
Alami was fired by BNP Paribas in 2019. He spent 12 years working for the bank after joining from HSBC in 2007. He was acknowledged to be very good at his job and when he left, he was head of equity derivative sales for Switzerland, Belgium and Luxembourg, earning $1.3m.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Alami didn't respond to multiple requests to comment for this article. Since leaving BNP Paribas, he's reinvented himself with a portfolio career and as CEO of Ora Technologies and Moroccan food delivery app, Kooul Maroc <https://apps.apple.com/us/app/kooul-food-delivery/id6502602776>. In this new existence, Alami seems cheerfully avuncular <https://www.linkedin.com/posts/kooulmaroc_nous-avons-lanc%C3%A9-une-nouvelle-version-de-activity-7455273746451329024-eACa?utm_source=share&utm_medium=member_desktop&rcm=ACoAAAC_n9ABKGyf486Jq5_52e4WCygx5h9EouQ> and highly chilled.
When he was at BNP Paribas, this may not always have been so. BNP Paribas fired Alami for allegedly bullying his subordinates </news/2022/04/associates-pjt-capital-exit-strategies>, such that one junior accused him of "emotional terrorism" and another declared, “I’ve never been so well paid, yet I’ve never felt so unhappy at work.” Alami always denied that he had humiliated or insulted colleagues and filed a $4m unfair dismissal claim against BNP Paribas for wrongly dismissing him. In 2022, he was awarded $1.8m in lost salary and bonuses after a judge agreed that he had been unfairly let go by the French bank. But yesterday, Alami lost that claim and the money on appeal after a judge ruled that his dismissal was warranted.
When Alami first left BNP Paribas, he found a new job in Switzerland, but said it paid him 60% less and gave him 60% less time with his family. Running Kooul Maroc looks like a better option.
In his original court case in 2022, Alami's lawyer noted <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2022-04-21/fired-bnp-boss-accused-of-emotional-terrorism-seeks-4-million?utm_content=business&utm_source=twitter&utm_campaign=socialflow-organic&utm_medium=social&cmpid=socialflow-twitter-business> that several of his colleagues had praised him for being kind and observed that he'd never been accused of aggressiveness during his annual appraisals. Alami himself said he'd simply been "lively" when he berated a BNP colleague for an apparent mistake. He said he was "never insulting or aggressive.”
At Kooul Maroc, Alami's new junior colleagues are more likely to include delivery riders and app developers than traders making errors that could cost $900k. This is probably a good thing. Other senior traders and salespeople who find themselves under daily stress may want to bear it mind as a possible alternative.
Speaking of yesterday's ruling, Alami's lawyer said: "This appeal decision violates the right to a fair trial and will be overturned by the Court of Cassation, as it is based solely on statements gathered anonymously and without any process to verify their accuracy or authorship. Furthermore, a review of this decision reveals that the Court of Appeal failed to consider the substantial evidence presented by Mr. Alami and failed to assess the severity of the discrimination he suffered due to his origins."
Follow me on X <https://x.com/MadameButcher>. Follow me on LinkedIn. <https://www.linkedin.com/in/sarahbutcherjournalist/>

item.title: The ex-Goldman Sachs VP on a mission to help students with banking internships
item.description:
Getting a first job at Goldman Sachs </news/how-to-get-a-job-goldman-sachs>is not easy. Nor is getting a first job at any other major bank. As students in the UK struggle to start their financial services careers, one former Goldman Sachs vice president (VP) is trying to make a difference.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
“The status quo in finance is that you need an internship to get an internship </news/internship-applications-banks>,” says Zhan Shi. “I personally don’t stand by that.”
Shi spent five years working in financial services after graduating from the University of Oxford. She’s a software developer and spent half that time working in engineering for Deutsche Bank and the other half working for Goldman Sachs in London. Shi left Goldman in 2024 and is now CEO of Career26, a platform for helping young people into banking careers.
Zhan is working with students like Liv Bowditch at UCL and Erasmus Yeo at Imperial.
Yeo has actually got an internship this summer at a private capital advisory firm. He says this internship didn’t come easy: “I made over 200 applications; it was incredibly stressful.”
Bowditch doesn’t have a finance internship. Like many students, she says she didn’t appreciate that she wanted to work in finance early enough </news/the-investment-banking-recruitment-process> in her first year at university. She therefore didn’t apply for the spring week </news/spring-week-bank> programmes that act as a slipstream to summer internships and then to graduate jobs. “I have a background in politics and I didn’t initially appreciate the intersection between politics, markets and policy,” she tells us.
Now that Bowditch does want to work in banking, it seems too late. She graduates next June and has spent over a year working as a part time communications advisor at the House of Commons. Bowditch is keen and able, but she hasn’t completed a banking internship previously and she doesn’t have an internship for this summer. This seems to be the end of her finance aspirations.
Shi would have it otherwise. “Career26 was set up to address a persistent gap: many students are motivated to pursue finance, but struggle to access or understand how the industry actually works beyond formal pathways such as internships,” she tells us. Career26 is organising a one-day student summit this October. There will be speakers from the likes of Goldman Sachs, BlackRock and Man Group.
The intention is to demystify finance careers, says Shi. The summit aims to be as accessible as possible to “younger, inexperienced students” and students from lower social economic backgrounds. It’s about skills, not “pedigree.”
“We want the kids to find role models early from the senior speakers, identify a career path that suits them based on real-person stories,” says Shi. Panellists are intentionally drawn from different roles across banking, trading, hedge funds and venture capital. After attending the summit and identifying a desired career path, students can revert to the platform and inform themselves further.
“We are trying to make the case that the current recruitment system isn’t working,” says Bowditch. "You need experience to get experience and there's a real problem of finance accidentally filtering out students from lower income backgrounds and universities where people are less aware of the recruitment timeline."
Whether attending a summit will compensate for the lack of an internship remains to be seen, but it can't do any harm.

item.title: Morning Coffee: Morgan Stanley’s most cutthroat office. Goldman Sachs bankers on $950k salaries need additional pleasing
item.description:
One of the most dangerous places in the world to stand is “between a junior banker </news/banks-weird-hierarchies-analysts-associates-vps-mds> and an opportunity”. Very few people get into the investment banking </news/what-is-an-investment-bank> industry because they want a measured career progression with work-life balance; </news/2023/05/banking-working-hours>they want big money </news/pay>, as quickly as possible. This is one of the aspects of the workload problem that doesn’t get talked about so much; when someone ends up working 110 hours in a week </news/junior-banker-pay-cost-of-living>, it’s easy to blame the managing director, but it’s just as likely that the real cause is that they were trying to outdo another associate who’s working 109 hours a week.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Morgan Stanley’s Budapest office <https://www.wsj.com/finance/banking/morgan-stanleys-investment-banking-program-in-budapest-hit-by-u-s-probe-72e03e93?mod=mhp> seems to be an example of this. The deal here was that MS hired a lot of ambitious young graduates across Europe, and told them that some, but not all of them would get the opportunity to transfer to London or New York. This, plus the fact that Hungary is at least six hours’ time difference from the USA, seems to have driven some pretty aggressive working practices. There are stories of MS juniors asking for permission to leave because they could see the sun rising over the Danube. (Morgan Stanley hasn’t commented yet, and it’s noticeable that nobody is alleging that the actual limits on hours were breached).
In fact, almost no one at Morgan Stanley's Budapest office achieved the desired London-New York transfer. The Wall Street Journal says only four bankers out of 40 have moved since 2024. When everyone wants the same thing and only 10% succeed, the competition is likely to be extreme.
It seems that some of the employees got grumpy (perhaps understandably) at the lifestyle and at the pay differential between the Budapest office and the teams they were supporting. Somebody made an anonymous claim that the Budapest team were doing considerably more advanced front-office </news/front-office-middle-office-back-office-banking> work than originally intended. The claim said people there might have gone beyond the level of involvement that their regulatory status qualified them to do.
Without any knowledge of whether these allegations are correct or not, there’s a certain inclination to say that this sounds like a problem of success rather than one of failure. In most cases, junior bankers don’t necessarily object to working long hours per se – they object to having their time wasted on unnecessary or boring low-level tasks. If they were being brought further and further in to the deals, that means that the teams in “the mothership” must have been growing to trust them. Which, as anyone who has been involved in an investment bank offshoring project will tell you, is not a trivial achievement.
Morgan Stanley's situation is not unique. When Citigroup set up its Malaga office <https://www.ft.com/content/611d9612-48f1-4286-935f-63ce5d758cdd>, promising work-life balance on lower pay, it ended up with exactly the same arms race of competitive overwork before being shut down. And it’s also interesting to note that many of the Malaguenos </news/citi-s-ex-malaga-analysts-are-finding-fine-jobs-away-from-the-vibes-of-london> ended up finding very good jobs at sovereign wealth funds and similar employers. In a couple of decades, we might find that the Mighty Morgan Stanley Magyars turn up at the very highest levels of European banking, and they’ll all be able to say they started on the very bottom floor.
Elsewhere – but possibly with the same underlying psychological structure, it’s not quite true that once you’ve made partner at Goldman Sachs </news/goldman-sachs-partners> you’ve reached the very top of the tree, earning upwards of $950k </news/goldman-sachs-partners> with super perks. Because there is also the management committee to get onto.
Officially, this isn’t an extra super-secret promotion; it’s just a committee of those partners who have taken on the additional responsibility of advising David Solomon </news/kathy-ruemmler-goldman-sachs>. But … there are some people who always think that if there’s a prize of any sort they want to win it and if there’s a title they want to get it. And people like that seem to be, unsurprisingly, more common at the top ranks of investment banks than in the average population.
Which is probably one reason why the management committee is quite big; despite past attempts to cut or restrict its membership, it recently grew from 45 to 47 members <https://www.ft.com/content/45df0f33-894c-4e0a-aca1-eea5c0cb21d0?syn-25a6b1a6=1> with the addition of Stephan Feldgoise, the global head of M&A </news/get-a-job-in-ma> and Josh Shiffrin, the head of risk for investment banking and trading.
The committee has added more than 20 people in the last 18 months. The FT says this is “an effort to elevate a new generation of leaders at Goldman but also part of a broader effort to retain top executives”. It certainly isn’t part of an effort to economise on biscuits.
Meanwhile …
Analysts will have to get used to saying “great six months, guys” as quarterly reporting becomes optional under new SEC rules. (FT <https://www.ft.com/content/3560d1e3-8271-453f-84d6-4a875b9efd98>)
Could this be why so many big US law firms are hiring so aggressively in London? Some private credit firms have begun to us the English bankruptcy courts to get more flexible restructurings agreed. (FT <https://www.ft.com/content/8ed5c126-a98f-4743-9b8f-086e26026569>)
Ken Griffin isn’t so keen on Zohran Mamdani’s New York and intends to make Citadel Miami even bigger. (Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-05/griffin-says-he-s-doubling-down-on-miami-amid-mamdani-feud>)
“On a panel about mergers and acquisitions, a managing director at the investment bank Jefferies wearily insisted that she didn’t want to talk about AI, and proceeded to do so for 15 minutes”. It sounds like the Milken Institute Conference isn’t as much fun this year as it used to be. (The American Prospect <https://prospect.org/2026/05/05/complacent-class-2026-milken-institute-global-conference/>)
Although, despite the “claustrophobic vibe”, many of the bankers there were still optimistic; JP Morgan’s Anu Aiyengar said that “Earlier, you would think about doing an M&A deal as adding to risk … now you think about doing M&A to mitigate the risk that is facing your business.” This includes “Geopolitical risk. AI risk. Tech-enablement risk. Supply chain risk [and] Demographic risk.”, so there’s practically no such thing as bad news anymore. (Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/newsletters/2026-05-05/why-the-m-a-animal-spirits-are-strong-at-milken-conference>)
LIV Golf began by recruiting Michael Klein to advise on its bid for the PGA, but is now retaining Michael Kramer (of Ducera Partners) to help it with financing options now that the Saudi wealth fund is not writing all the checks. (LIVGolf <https://www.livgolf.com/news/liv-golf-retains-investment-bank-ducera-partners-to-drive-long-term-capital-strategy-2026>)
Cyan Banister is a “professional daydreamer” who thinks that “Pokemon Go was the closest we ever came to world peace”. But she was also an early stage investor in Uber, SpaceX and DeepMind, so maybe her approach to imagining the future ought to be taken seriously. (Generalist <https://www.generalist.com/p/investing-like-a-mystic-cyan-banister?source=queue>)
